title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Spot Prices See Three-Week Streak of Weekly Gains; Type 1 Trade Volumes Dip
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(30th June â€“ 6th July)
  • A total of 361,666 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 9.50% fall week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 26.34% fall WoW as 1,094,561 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $44.57/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 3.24% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 8.31% in price, reaching $51.10/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 1.09% WoW; on 8th July, the spot price stood at $42.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $46.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 3.62% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 4.81% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the past week, prices for Type 1 trades have declined, while spot prices have seen a consistent rise for three consecutive weeks. The House of Representatives has officially passed the One Big Beautiful Bill, introduced by President Trump. The Bill now awaits his signature to become law. Industry groups, including the Renewable Fuels Association (RFA), have expressed strong support for the Bill, particularly applauding its key tax provisions designed to benefit U.S. ethanol producers. Additionally, on 8th July, 2025, the U.S. EPA held a public hearing to gather input on its proposed Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) rule for 2026 and 2027. The proposal outlines an 8% increase in the 2026 Renewable Volume Obligation (RVO), setting it at 24.02 Billion Renewable Identification Numbers (RINs), and a 2% increase for 2027, bringing it to 24.46 Billion RINs. The proposal includes a 15 Billion gallon ethanol target and boosts biobased diesel RVOs. While the biofuel industry generally supports the proposal, concerns remain regarding small refinery exemptions (SREs) and the handling of export markets.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 3.24% WoW rise, increasing from $43.17 to $44.57 this week, and all non-zero credit prices increased by 8.31% WoW, from $47.18 to $51.10. Type 1 trades saw 361,666 credits exchanged, reflecting a 9.50% fall WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a 26.34% fall WoW, reaching 1,094,561 credits. [caption id="attachment_600146" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 1.09% WoW, reaching $47.44/MT. On 8th July, the spot price was $42.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $46.00/MT as of 8th July. [caption id="attachment_600147" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 67 trades were reported in the market, with 8 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for two Type 2 transactions at volumes of 10,000 and 5,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $39.86 per credit of Type 1 reported for 120,000 credits, which was also the largest volume transfers. The smallest volume transfers of 860 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $45.00. [caption id="attachment_600148" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot Type 1 price stood at $46.70 in July so far, up from $44.06 in June. Trade volumes for the month of July stood at 0.54 M. [caption id="attachment_600149" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 30th Â June â€“ 6th July, total VPT recorded a 45.23% increase WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. [caption id="attachment_600150" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 3.62% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 4.81% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • The One Big Beautiful Bill is on its Way to President Trumpâ€™s Desk: The House of Representatives just officially PASSED the One Big Beautiful Bill, giving final approval to President Donald J. Trumpâ€™s landmark legislation in what is being called the â€œbiggest legislative win of President Trumpâ€™s two terms.â€ Now, the largest middle-class tax cut in American history and so much more is on its way to President Trumpâ€™s desk.
  • RFA Applauds Passage of OBBBA, Tax Policy Clarity for U.S. Ethanol Producers: The Renewable Fuels Association (RFA) applauded the inclusion of several key tax provisions that will enhance the role of the U.S. ethanol industry in contributing to American energy security and innovation.
  • Biofuel Groups Testify on EPAâ€™s 2026, 2027 RFS Proposed Rule: The U.S. EPA on July 8 hosted virtual public hearing to gather input on the agencyâ€™s recently released proposed rule to set 2026 and 2027 Renewable Fuel Standard renewable volume obligations (RVOs). Members of the biofuel industry were among those to offer testimony during the event. The proposed rulemaking, released by the EPA on June 13, would set the 2026 RVO at 24.02 billion renewable identification numbers (RINs), a nearly 8% increase when compared to the 2025 RVO.
  • Tesla launches Oasis Supercharger with solar farm and Off-grid Batteries: Tesla has launched its new â€œOasisâ€ Supercharger station in Lost Hills, California, featuring 168 EV chargersâ€”84 now operationalâ€”and powered entirely by 11 MW of ground-mounted solar panels and 10 Tesla Megapacks with 39 MWh of battery storage. Spanning 30 acres, the station can operate off-grid and is Teslaâ€™s first fully energy-independent charging location, fulfilling a long-standing vision for sustainable EV infrastructure. The project marks one of the largest Supercharger deployments to date.
